article_title: Japan Airlines Begins Two-Year Humanoid Robot Trial at Haneda, Pairing Unitree G1 Units With Ground Crews to Confront a Labor Shortage
summary: Body cites Aviation A2Z (aviationa2z.com) seven times but the URL is missing from article.sources — provenance break that the automated check did not catch

findings.message: Body cites a URL not present in article.sources
findings.details: The body markdown links to https://aviationa2z.com/index.php/2026/04/29/this-airline-is-using-humanoid-robots-at-tokyo-haneda-airport/ in seven places, supporting specific factual claims (two-robot initial deployment, cargo container + lever locking duties, three-year phased exercise framing, 4,000 ground-handling staff figure, future expansion roadmap including baggage loading + cabin cleaning + GSE handling). However, aviationa2z.com is NOT included in article.sources, which lists six other URLs. The Chief Editor's source-snapshot fetcher only fetches URLs in article.sources, so the Aviation A2Z claims have no committed snapshot in sources/2026-05/ and are unverifiable from the provenance chain. Either the URL must be added to article.sources (requires resubmission since the existing submission is signed and immutable) or every Aviation A2Z citation must be re-attributed to a sourced URL or removed.
newatlas.com, aeroxplorer.com, japantoday.com, futurism.com — none on config/source_allowlist.txt

content_preview.title: Japan Airlines Begins Two-Year Humanoid Robot Trial at Haneda, Pairing Unitree G1 Units With Ground Crews to Confront a Labor Shortage
content_preview.summary: JAL Ground Service and GMO AI & Robotics will deploy humanoid robots on the apron at Tokyo's Haneda starting in May, in what local press call Japan's first such airport trial.
content_preview.body_excerpt:
## Overview

Japan Airlines and GMO Internet Group announced on April 27 that they will begin a multi-year demonstration trial of humanoid robots on the apron at Tokyo's Haneda Airport beginning in May, according to [The Register](https://www.theregister.com/2026/04/29/japan_airport_robot_trial/). The pilot, run jointly by JAL Ground Service and GMO AI & Robotics, will start with two China-built humanoid units performing cargo-handling tasks alongside human ground crews and is scheduled to conti...

recommendations: Consider adding trusted domains to config/source_allowlist.txt

editor_notes.content_quality: Substantive News article (1,025 words) on a real April 27 JAL/GMO announcement. Strong cross-source structure and honest 'What We Don't Know' section flagging a real reporting divergence on supplier identity (Unitree G1 per IE/New Atlas vs. GMO lineup per The Register). The substantive content is fine — the issue is procedural.

source_verification.https://www.theregister.com/2026/04/29/j: source-0.html — Verified key claims: April 27 announcement, JAL Ground Service + GMO AI & Robotics, 'two-year testing period', JAL's 'myriad pieces of equipment' rationale quote, 'sustainable operational structure through labor savings' quote, GMO offering a humanoid lineup with one outdoor-capable model.
source_verification.https://interestingengineering.com/ai-ro: source-1.html — Verified: Unitree G1, 1.32m / 35kg / 23 DoF / 3D LiDAR / depth cameras / 9000 mAh / two-hour runtime, 'sustainable operational structure' identical statement.
source_verification.https://newatlas.com/ai-humanoids/humano: source-2.html — Verified: Unitree G1 supplier identification, 60M+ Haneda passengers/year.
source_verification.https://aeroxplorer.com/articles/japan-a: source-3.html — Verified: ~4,000 JAL ground-handling staff, 'complementary to human workers rather than as replacements' framing, three-year phased verification.
source_verification.https://japantoday.com/category/tech/jal: source-4.html — Verified: through-2028 timeline (Kyodo), Tomohiro Uchida 'back-end operations still rely heavily on human labor' quote, Yoshiteru Suzuki 'inevitably reduce workers' burden' quote, 'two to three hours' runtime caveat, future expansion to cabin cleaning.
source_verification.https://futurism.com/robots-and-machines: source-5.html — Verified: 60M+ Haneda passengers, 'barely touches' container demo observation, Uchida quote via BBC reporting, broader Chinese humanoid market context.
source_verification.https://aviationa2z.com/index.php/2026/0: MISSING FROM article.sources. The Chief Editor's snapshot fetcher does not download URLs that are not in article.sources, so this URL has no snapshot in sources/2026-05/. Seven specific claims attributed to it in the body are therefore unverifiable from the provenance chain.

editor_notes.factual_accuracy: The factual content of the body is mostly verifiable against the six sources that ARE in article.sources. The blocking issue is procedural: Aviation A2Z appears as an inline citation seven times but is not in article.sources, breaking the provenance contract that every cited URL must be in the sources array (and therefore have a committed snapshot under sources/<YYYY-MM>/<slug>/).
editor_notes.tone_assessment: Neutral and analytical. Honest about the IE/New-Atlas vs. The Register supplier divergence rather than picking one and hiding the conflict.
concerns: Aviation A2Z (aviationa2z.com) is cited inline 7 times in the body but missing from article.sources — provenance break. Specific claims that rest in part on this orphan source: two-robot initial deployment, cargo container / lever locking task list, three-year phased exercise framing, 4,000 ground-handling staff figure (also corroborated by AeroXplorer), future expansion roadmap.

The v3.8.0 pre-submission Step 5a was supposed to catch orphan URLs (every inline link must trace to a research-log entry whose URL is in article.sources). It missed this case. The chief:review automated 'sources_referenced' check also missed it — the script verifies only that every article.sources URL is referenced in the body, not the inverse.
recommendations: Resubmit with aviationa2z.com added to the article.sources array (requires a new submission since the current one is signed and immutable). Alternatively, re-attribute every Aviation A2Z citation to a URL that is in the sources array (most claims can be moved to AeroXplorer or Japan Today, which carry overlapping content), then resubmit.

Pipeline-level follow-up: extend chief:review's 'sources_referenced' check to also verify the inverse — every URL appearing as a Markdown link target in body_markdown must be in article.sources. This would catch orphan-source cases automatically rather than relying on manual review.
editor_notes.overall_assessment: REQUEST_CHANGES. The article's substantive content is fine but seven inline citations point to a URL (aviationa2z.com) that is not in article.sources, breaking the provenance chain. The bot must resubmit with the URL in the sources array, or with the orphan citations re-attributed/removed. This is the same kind of pipeline-integrity issue (orphan source URL) that v3.8.0 Step 5a was designed to catch — it slipped through here and should be tightened in a future iteration.
